title: Probing the Chirality of Trigonal Selenium and Tellurium by Spin and Orbital Hall Effects
authors: Yuting Xiong, Yingjie Hu, Wei Ren, Heng Gao
abstract: Chiral crystals exhibit enantiomer-dependent transport phenomena that generate pure spin or orbital currents, while the handedness sensitivity of spin and orbital Hall conductivities (SHC/OHC) remains insufficiently understood. Using first-principles calculations, we demonstrate that trigonal selenium and tellurium -- prototypical chiral semiconductors -- exhibit opposite signs of the SHC/OHC tensor elements $σ_{yx}^{S_y}$ and $σ_{yx}^{L_y}$ between their left- and right-handed enantiomers. This behavior originates from the mirror operation relating the two structures, described by space groups $P3_221$ (left-handed) and $P3_121$ (right-handed). Although both enantiomers share identical band structures and four nonzero SHC/OHC tensor components, $σ_{yx}^{S_y}$ and $σ_{yx}^{L_y}$ reverse sign due to the antisymmetric transformation of the spin/orbital Berry curvature under the $M_{xy}$ mirror operation. More generally, for mirror-related enantiomorphic structures, selected SHC/OHC tensor components can exhibit symmetry-governed sign reversal. For trigonal Se and Te, the calculated signs of these components can be directly correlated with the left- and right-handed structures under the chosen coordinate convention. These results clarify the symmetry origin of handedness-dependent SHC/OHC and suggest a possible route for correlating measurable SHC/OHC signals with structural handedness in specific chiral materials.
summary: This paper uses first-principles calculations to show that the spin and orbital Hall effects in trigonal Selenium and Tellurium are sensitive to the crystal's handedness. It identifies that specific transport tensor components change sign between enantiomers because of how the Berry curvature transforms under mirror symmetry. This provides a potential method to determine the structural chirality of materials through measurable transport signals.
main_problem: Understanding the symmetry origin of the handedness (chirality) sensitivity of spin Hall conductivity (SHC) and orbital Hall conductivity (OHC) in chiral crystals.
main_result: The researchers demonstrated that the SHC and OHC tensor components (specifically sigma_yx^Sy and sigma_yx^Ly) reverse sign between left- and right-handed enantiomers of trigonal Se and Te due to the antisymmetric transformation of the spin/orbital Berry curvature under a mirror operation.
method: First-principles calculations using Density Functional Theory (DFT) with PBE and HSE06 functionals, including spin-orbit coupling, and the Kubo formula for calculating Hall responses via Berry curvature.
model_or_system: The study focuses on trigonal Selenium (Se) and Tellurium (Te), which are prototypical chiral semiconductors featuring infinite helical screw structures along the c-axis with space groups P3_221 and P3_121.
key_observables: Spin Hall Conductivity (SHC) tensor elements, Orbital Hall Conductivity (OHC) tensor elements, and Spin/Orbital Berry Curvatures.
important_parameters: Space groups P3_221 and P3_121, mirror operation M_xy, and the spin-orbit coupling strength.
main_assumptions: The sign reversal is a symmetry-governed consequence for specific mirror-related enantiomorphic structures and may not be a universal criterion for all chiral crystals.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Schematic of left/right-handed crystal structures and band structures; Fig 2: SHC tensor components and energy-dependent variation showing sign reversal; Fig 4: k-resolved spin Berry curvature; Fig 5: OHC tensor components and energy dependence; Fig 6: Schematic of tensor transformations under mirror symmetry.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of chirality-dependent transport, describes the structural and electronic properties of Se and Te, presents the symmetry analysis of tensor transformations, details the DFT and Kubo-based calculation results for SHC and OHC, and concludes with the implications for identifying structural handedness.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Crystal structures of chiral Se/Te. (a) and (b) show the schematic structures of left- and right-handed Se/Te, re- spectively. θ, r, and R in (a) denote the bond angle, intra- screw distance, and inter-screw distance, respectively. The 3D helical arrows that explicitly indicate the screw sense (clock- wise/counterclockwise) mirror operation Mxy (yellow plane), respectively. The band structures of Se (c) and Te (d). HSE06 including SOC (red); PBE without SOC (cyan).

FIG. 2. The non-zero independent SHC tensors σSz xy , σ Sy zx and σ Sy xz of left- and right-chiral trigonal (a) Se and Te (b). The comparison of σ Sy yx components for left- and right-handed (c) Se and (d) Te.

FIG. 3. The band-projected spin Berry curvature (top) Ω Sy n,yx(k) on a log scale, k-resolved spin Berry curvature (bot- tom) Ω Sy yx(k) in (a) left- and (b) right-handed Se. The Ω Sy yx(k) at the Fermi level on a log scale in a slice of kz = 0.5×(2π/c) for (c) left- and (d) right-handed Te.

FIG. 4. The band-projected spin Berry curvature (top) Ω Sy n,yx(k) on a log scale, k-resolved spin Berry curvature (bot- tom) Ω Sy yx(k) in (a) left- and (b) right-handed Se. The slice plane kz = 0.5 × (2π/c) is displayed with yellow color in (c). The Ω Sy yx(k) at the Fermi level on a log scale in a slice of kz = 0.5 × (2π/c) for (d) left- and (e) right-handed Se.
FIG. 6. Schematics of the transformation relationship of Sk and Lk under mirror symmetry operation Mxy, where k de- notes the direction of S or L along the x, y, or z axis.
